programmes.title: Compression-as-Intelligence
programmes.hard_core: A model's average lossless-compression rate over a held-out text corpus is approximately linearly related to its average performance on broad downstream benchmarks, across model families and across multiple orders of magnitude of model scale.

claims.text: LLMs achieve competitive lossless compression on text.
claims.refuting: none

claims.text: LLMs achieve competitive lossless compression on non-text modalities (audio, images).
claims.refuting: none at scale

claims.text: Compression performance linearly predicts benchmark performance within a model family.
claims.refuting: none of comparable scale

claims.text: Compression performance linearly predicts benchmark performance *across* model families, including reasoning-tuned models.
claims.refuting: the strong form has not been re-tested on o1 / R1 / s1 cohorts at publication time; reasoning-tuned models are obvious risk cases

claims.text: Improving compression is sufficient for improving downstream capability.
claims.refuting: tool-using and reasoning-tuned models complicate the causal direction

claims.text: Compression as Solomonoff induction is the *unique* explanation for LLM generality.
claims.status: ⚪ Open (a strong claim with no clean empirical test yet)
claims.refuting: Huh et al.; Platonic Representation programme

claims.text: Trained networks pack more features than they have neurons.
claims.supporting: Elhage et al. 2022 (toy); Bricken et al. 2023 (real)
claims.refuting: none at present

claims.text: At least some features are linearly readable from activations.
claims.supporting: Park, Choe, Veitch 2023; broad probing literature
claims.refuting: trivially false in its strongest form (some features are clearly non-linear)

claims.text: The features SAEs find are *interpretable*, not artifacts of SAE training.
claims.supporting: Bricken et al. 2023; Cunningham et al. 2023
claims.refuting: Kantamneni et al. 2025; concerns about SAE-dependent feature shape

claims.text: Features are *universal*: the same features appear across model families and scales.
claims.supporting: partial: induction heads (Olsson et al.); shared monosemantic features in Bricken et al.
claims.refuting: no large-scale cross-family universality study yet at publication time

claims.text: Linear representation is the *unique* useful description; non-linear alternatives are no better.
claims.refuting: Tamkin et al. 2023 (discrete codebooks); various non-linear-probe results

claims.text: SAE features are *causally* (not just correlationally) what the model uses.
claims.refuting: causal validation is per-feature and patchy

claims.text: Polysemanticity is fully resolved at the feature level (i.e., SAE features are reliably monosemantic).
claims.refuting: follow-up "feature splitting" / "feature merging" papers show width-dependent polysemanticity remains

claims.text: SAE methods improve predictably along a reconstruction-vs-sparsity Pareto frontier with architectural improvements (gated, JumpReLU, top-K).
claims.supporting: Rajamanoharan et al. (gated) 2024; Rajamanoharan et al. (JumpReLU) 2024; Gao et al. 2024
claims.refuting: n/a — robustly replicated

claims.text: Number of recoverable features grows predictably with model and SAE scale (scaling laws).
claims.refuting: scope: top-K SAEs on GPT-4-class activations

claims.text: Specific safety-relevant attributes (truthfulness, language identity) are linearly represented.
claims.refuting: n/a in scope

programmes.title: Circuits & the Biology of LLMs
programmes.hard_core: For a range of well-specified tasks, the model's behavior on the task is supported by a small, identifiable sub-graph of attention heads, MLP neurons (or SAE features), and edges between them; the sub-graph can be discovered by causal interventions (ablation, activation patching) and is reproducible across runs and, in many cases, across model families.

claims.text: Specific narrow tasks (IOI, induction, modular addition) admit small, causally-validated circuits.
claims.supporting: Wang et al. 2022; Olsson et al. 2022; Nanda et al. 2023
claims.refuting: none

claims.text: Circuit discovery can be (semi-)automated.
claims.refuting: scaling-to-frontier-model concerns

claims.text: Circuit motifs are universal across model families.
claims.refuting: universality of less-canonical motifs is weak; few cross-family replications at scale

claims.text: "Circuits" generalize to *general* model behavior, not only narrow tasks.
claims.supporting: Anthropic biology-of-LLM work attempts this; community-survey (Sharkey et al. 2025) marks it open
claims.refuting: this is the hard version of the claim

claims.text: Faithfulness metrics for circuits are well-calibrated.
claims.refuting: Hanna et al. 2024 — many published circuits are over-credited under stricter metrics

claims.text: Some behaviors are mediated by a *single direction* rather than a multi-component circuit.
claims.refuting: sharpens but does not refute 03-A

claims.text: Circuits *explain* (vs. merely describe) model behavior on out-of-distribution inputs.
claims.refuting: a serious OOD-behavior prediction benchmark for circuits does not yet exist

claims.text: Circuit analyses on small models transfer to larger / longer-trained models.
claims.refuting: cross-family transfer still partial

claims.text: Feature-level circuits are extractable and editable at frontier scale.
claims.refuting: independent replication of frontier-scale attribution graphs pending

claims.text: Single residual-stream directions can mediate complex behaviors (refusal, truthfulness).
claims.supporting: Arditi et al. 2024; Li et al. 2023
claims.refuting: scope: safety-relevant attributes — not yet shown for arbitrary tasks

programmes.title: ICL as Implicit Bayes / Meta-Learning
programmes.hard_core: Pretraining on data with latent-task structure makes the transformer behave, at inference time, as if it were doing posterior inference over the latent-task variable. As context grows, the model's predictions track the Bayes-optimal predictor under the implicit prior defined by the pretraining distribution.

claims.text: On synthetic mixture-of-HMM data, ICL tracks Bayes-optimal prediction as context grows.
claims.refuting: none in scope

claims.text: For linear ICL tasks, transformers implement gradient descent / ridge regression / Bayesian inference.
claims.supporting: Akyürek et al.; Garg et al.; von Oswald et al.
claims.refuting: beyond the in-scope tasks the claim is open

claims.text: Transformers select among multiple in-context algorithms based on context.
claims.refuting: empirical breadth is open

claims.text: ICL on natural language closely tracks Bayes-optimal predictions.
claims.refuting: systematic deviations on rare/OOD tasks

claims.text: ICL is the asymptotic equilibrium of next-token training on task-mixed data.
claims.refuting: Singh et al. 2023 — ICL can be transient; in-weights learning supplants it

claims.text: The mechanistic implementation of ICL is captured by induction heads / similar circuits (cross-link to 03).
claims.refuting: induction heads explain part, not all, of ICL on natural text

claims.text: ICL behavior is Bayes-invariant to surface-form changes in the prompt.
claims.refuting: Min et al. 2022 — random-label demonstrations work; surface-form sensitivities are pervasive

claims.text: ICL is implemented (in part) via compression of demonstrations into a residual-stream task vector.
claims.supporting: Hendel et al. 2023; Todd et al. 2023
claims.refuting: scope: single-task ICL setups

claims.text: For relational tasks, LMs implement word2vec-style additive vector arithmetic.
claims.refuting: scope: relational tasks specifically

programmes.title: Emergence and Reasoning
programmes.hard_core: for some downstream tasks, model performance is approximately at chance until a critical scale of model parameters or training compute, after which it improves sharply; this discontinuity is not predictable from smaller-scale runs under any continuous metric.

claims.text: Wei-style strong emergence: capability is genuinely zero below scale $S^*$ and positive above, *unpredictably* from smaller runs.
claims.refuting: Schaeffer et al. 2023 (metric-substitution refutation)

claims.text: Under common (multi-choice / exact-match) metrics, some capability curves *are* sharp as a function of scale.
claims.refuting: not contested as description; only as interpretation

claims.text: Training-time grokking phase changes have continuous internal causes (mechanistically explainable).
claims.supporting: Nanda et al. 2023; Olsson et al. 2022
claims.refuting: n/a

claims.text: Test-time compute scaling produces capability gains that look discontinuous in *capability-per-prompt* but continuous in *compute-per-prompt*.
claims.supporting: Snell et al. 2024; OpenAI o1; DeepSeek-R1; s1
claims.refuting: exact magnitude and task-coverage debated

claims.text: "Emergence" in the strict complex-systems sense (order parameter discontinuities) is a real phenomenon in LLMs.
claims.refuting: no clean order-parameter result yet

claims.text: Reasoning capability can be elicited by small post-training datasets + inference-time control.
claims.refuting: n/a in the cited scope
